revision_history: v1 (2026-05-17): initial draft, autonomous mode. Live web verification not available in the runtime; brief and stage1 marked uncertainties via needs_verification for Stage 3 (ChatGPT) to chase as priority. Conservative framing used throughout — no specific Malaysian company is named as a diversion actor, only those publicly named in the Singapore SPF charges (which the brief itself flags for confirmation).
_provenance.discipline: Process-not-substance. The story is the structural gap between Malaysia's announced data-centre FDI thesis and the US export-control envelope that sits over the chips those data centres run on — not a prediction that any specific Malaysian company will be sanctioned. No 3R content.

headline: Singapore charged three over Nvidia chips routed via Malaysia. Johor kept building.
context: On 27 February 2025, Singapore charged three men with fraud tied to the sale of servers carrying Nvidia AI chips that moved from Singapore to Malaysia. Johor has since approved more data-centre capacity. The US export-control framework that governs those chips is still being rewritten.

cards.big: On 27 February 2025, Singapore charged three men over the sale of servers carrying Nvidia AI chips. The route ran Singapore to Malaysia.
cards.sub: US prosecutors are investigating whether high-end chips that landed in Malaysia were diverted to a Chinese AI firm. Johor approved more data-centre capacity in the same quarter.
sources: Singapore Police Force press release, 27 Feb 2025 — three men charged with fraud / false representation tied to server sales [PRIMARY — Stage 3 to confirm exact title and URL]
Singapore Ministry of Home Affairs, Minister K. Shanmugam's public statement (Feb/Mar 2025) that the servers moved from Singapore to Malaysia [PRIMARY — Stage 3 to confirm date and forum]
Reuters / Bloomberg / WSJ secondary reporting on the case and on parallel US DOJ and Commerce Department investigations [SECONDARY — Stage 3 to surface specific articles]

cards.big: Washington classifies Malaysia as a 'Tier 2' diversion-risk country for AI chips — annual caps and validated-end-user rules, even after the rule was rescinded.
cards.sub: BIS issued the AI Diffusion Rule on 13 January 2025 placing Malaysia in Tier 2. Commerce Secretary Lutnick rescinded it on 13 May 2025, two days before its scheduled effect — and replaced it with case-by-case licensing plus an explicit diversion warning.
sources: BIS, 'Framework for Artificial Intelligence Diffusion,' Interim Final Rule, 90 Fed. Reg. 4544 (15 January 2025) — defines Tier 1/2/3 and places Malaysia in Tier 2 [PRIMARY]
US Department of Commerce / BIS, rescission of the Diffusion Rule, 13 May 2025; Secretary Lutnick announcement [PRIMARY — Stage 3 to confirm exact Federal Register cite]
15 CFR Parts 730-774 (US Export Administration Regulations); ECCN 3A090 covers advanced AI accelerators [PRIMARY]

cards.big: Johor has more than 80 approved data-centre projects with announced capacity above 5 GW — roughly a quarter of Peninsular Malaysia's peak electricity demand.
cards.sub: Microsoft (USD 2.2 B announced May 2024), Google (USD 2 B announced May 2024), AWS, ByteDance and YTL are anchor tenants. The investment thesis assumes uninterrupted access to US-controlled AI chips.
sources: MIDA Annual Investment Performance Reports 2023, 2024; MITI ministerial statements on Johor DC approvals [PRIMARY — Stage 3 to confirm current approval count]
DC Byte / Cushman & Wakefield / Knight Frank Asia-Pacific data-centre reports 2024-2025 — capacity tracker estimates [SECONDARY — commercial interest disclosed]
Suruhanjaya Tenaga (Energy Commission), Peninsular Malaysia Electricity Supply Industry Outlook — peak demand reference [PRIMARY]
Microsoft news release, 'Microsoft to Invest $2.2 Billion to Fuel Malaysia's Cloud and AI Transformation,' 2 May 2024 [PRIMARY]
Google blog, 'A $2 billion investment to support Malaysia's digital transformation,' 30 May 2024 [PRIMARY]

cards.big: MITI said it would investigate any Malaysian company named in a US diversion probe. The investigation list has not been made public.
cards.sub: Trade Minister Tengku Zafrul gave that commitment in March 2025. The Strategic Trade Act 2010 carries up to RM 1 million in corporate fines and five years' jail for unlicensed export of listed strategic items.
sources: MITI ministerial statement, March 2025 — commitment to investigate any company named in a US probe [NEEDS VERIFICATION on exact date and forum]
Malaysia Strategic Trade Act 2010 (Act 708), Attorney General's Chambers consolidated text — penalty provisions [PRIMARY]

cards.big: The question is not whether Malaysia is selling chips to China. The question is whether Johor is the freeport on the route, and what happens if Washington decides it is.
sources: Editorial reframe. Rotates the reader's mental model from 'Malaysia is winning the AI-investment race' to 'Malaysia is operating inside a US export-control envelope it does not set'. Anchored on the Singapore SPF charges, the US DOJ/Commerce reporting, and the structural fact that the chips powering Johor's announced capacity are US-controlled items under EAR.

cards.big: Like building the largest port on a known smuggling route, then asking the customs office across the border to keep clearing it.
cards.sub: Johor's data-centre build-out depends on the same US export-control regime that just charged three men in the country next door.
sources: Editorial analogy. Mirrors verified findings: Singapore Feb 2025 charges; BIS Tier 2 classification and case-by-case licensing; >80 approved Johor DC projects.

cards.big: Three men were charged in Singapore. The chips were headed for Malaysia. Johor's response was to approve more capacity. The bill arrives when Washington decides what to do about it.
sources: Editorial synthesis. Anchored on: SPF charges 27 Feb 2025; BIS Diffusion Rule (13 Jan 2025) and rescission (13 May 2025); MIDA / MITI public DC-approval statements; Energy Commission peak-demand figure; Microsoft and Google announcement releases.

BIS Federal Register notices (13 Jan 2025 Diffusion Rule; May 2025 rescission), 15 CFR Parts 730-774 (EAR), Singapore Police Force release (27 Feb 2025), Singapore Ministry of Home Affairs (Shanmugam statement), Singapore Penal Code, Singapore Strategic Goods (Control) Act 2002, Malaysia Strategic Trade Act 2010, Customs Act 1967, MIDA Annual Investment Performance Reports 2023-2024, MITI ministerial statements, Microsoft and Google corporate news releases (May 2024), YTL Power Bursa filings, Suruhanjaya Tenaga Electricity Supply Industry Outlook, Reuters / Bloomberg / WSJ / The Edge / FMT secondary reporting, DC Byte / Cushman & Wakefield / Knight Frank Asia-Pacific DC capacity trackers, CSIS Wadhwa Center analysis on the Diffusion Rule
confidence: Medium-High on the legal and statutory framework (EAR primary text, BIS Federal Register Diffusion Rule, Strategic Trade Act 2010, Customs Act). Medium-High on the Singapore case existence and the Singapore-to-Malaysia route claim (multi-outlet reporting plus minister statement). Medium on operational specifics (precise volume, identity of any Malaysian recipient company) — these are not publicly confirmed and the cards do not name a Malaysian company. Medium on the >80 Johor DC project count and >5 GW capacity (a range exists across MITI / MIDA / industry trackers; brief uses a conservative middle figure). Low on the post-cutoff status (Feb 2026 - May 2026) of US enforcement actions and the latest BIS guidance text — Stage 3 priority.
needs_verification: Exact Federal Register citation and effective text for the May 2025 BIS rescission of the AI Diffusion Rule, and any subsequent BIS chip-licensing guidance issued before May 2026.

Singapore Police Force charge-sheet wording: exact section of the Penal Code charged, exact value of suspect transactions (S$390M vs US$390M discrepancy in secondary reporting), and exact identities of the three accused.

Whether the SPF charge sheet, the AGC Singapore filing, or Minister Shanmugam's statement names a specific Chinese AI firm (DeepSeek or otherwise) as the suspected end-user. Cards should not name DeepSeek unless this is verified.
Tengku Zafrul / MITI March 2025 statement: exact date, forum, and wording of the commitment to investigate any named Malaysian company.

Current MIDA / MITI approved-Johor-DC-project count and announced GW capacity figure (the brief uses '>80 projects, >5 GW' as a mid-range conservative figure; Stage 3 to confirm against the most recent MIDA Annual Investment Performance Report).

Whether any Malaysian data-centre operator has been publicly named in a US BIS, DOJ, or Commerce Department enforcement action between Feb 2025 and May 2026. The cards explicitly do not name one because none is verified.
source_notes: v1 was generated in a runtime without live web search, so the brief and the cards both flag specific items (SPF charge-sheet wording, BIS post-rescission Federal Register cite, MITI March 2025 statement date, current Johor approval count) for Stage 3 to verify against primary sources before publication. The currency of the ~390M figure differs across secondary reports (Reuters has used US$390M; CNA and Singapore-government communications S$390M); the cards therefore omit the figure rather than commit to a wrong one. The Chinese-AI-firm identity (some reports name DeepSeek) is not asserted in cards because it has not been verified against the charge sheet. The hook intentionally describes only what is publicly verifiable: three men charged, the Singapore-to-Malaysia route, and parallel US investigation reporting. The reframe and analogy are clearly labelled editorial in their sources entries; they do not introduce unverified specifics. The view's specifics ('three men were charged in Singapore', 'chips were headed for Malaysia', 'Johor approved more capacity') all trace to primary or near-primary sources cited above.

_self_check.every_claim_traces_to_source: Every numeric and named claim in cards traces to a primary or near-primary source listed above OR is explicitly flagged in needs_verification. No card asserts a specific Chinese AI firm by name; no card names a specific Malaysian recipient company; no card asserts a USD-denominated currency figure.
_self_check.overclaim_check: Cards do not assert that any Malaysian DC operator has been sanctioned, indicted, or named (none has been publicly). Cards do not assert that the diversion occurred — only that Singapore charged three men with fraud tied to server sales, that US prosecutors are investigating, and that the Singapore-to-Malaysia route is acknowledged by the Singapore minister. The reframe is framed as a question ('what happens if Washington decides'), not as a prediction.
_self_check.underclaim_check: Cards do not soften the verified findings. Singapore DID charge three men. BIS DID classify Malaysia as Tier 2 in the Diffusion Rule. The rule WAS rescinded 13 May 2025 and replaced with case-by-case licensing. MITI DID commit to investigation. None of these are softened to 'critics say' or 'reportedly'.
_self_check.misleading_framing_check: Hook leads with what is on the public record (the charges) before the reframe. The capacity card establishes Johor's scale BEFORE the export-control card lands the constraint, so the reader sees both sides simultaneously. The reframe rotates the model rather than adding evidence. The analogy is editorial and clearly labelled.
_self_check.unverified_detail_check: No specific Malaysian company name. No specific Chinese AI firm name. No specific dollar value of the Singapore transactions. No specific volume of chips. All four of these are tempting concretizers and all four are intentionally absent until Stage 3 verifies. The figures present (3 men, 13 Jan 2025, 13 May 2025, 80+ projects, 5 GW, USD 2.2B Microsoft, USD 2B Google, 27 Feb 2025) all trace to public primary or near-primary sources.
_self_check.3R_special_caution: No race, religion, or royalty content. Pure trade-security-economic story.
_self_check.process_not_people_check: Named individuals: K. Shanmugam (Singapore minister, official capacity), Tengku Zafrul (then MITI minister, official capacity), Howard Lutnick (US Commerce Secretary, official capacity). No named individual is characterised as bad-faith. The three accused in Singapore are referenced only as a count ('three men') because the charges are not the story — the structural exposure is.
_self_check.anger_anxiety_check: Dominant emotion target: anxiety-of-precedent — Johor's investment thesis sits on a chip-supply framework the US is rewriting. Not sadness. Not anger-at-people. Anchored on structure, not on alleged wrongdoing by any named MY actor.

pa_score_rationale: Capped at 78, not higher, because: (a) Stage 1 generated without live web verification — several specifics (BIS post-rescission Federal Register cite, MITI March 2025 statement date, current MIDA approval count) are flagged for Stage 3 to verify before publication; (b) the S$390M / US$390M currency discrepancy is unresolved in v1 and the cards intentionally omit the figure rather than assert a wrong one; (c) no Malaysian DC operator is named, by design — but this also means the cards rely on the structural-exposure argument rather than a named-actor story, which a stricter Stage 3 may flag as needing a verified Malaysian actor anchor; (d) post-cutoff status (Feb 2026 - May 2026) of BIS enforcement is unknown to the v1 generator. Strong: legal/statutory citations to EAR, BIS Diffusion Rule, Strategic Trade Act 2010 are primary; Singapore case existence and Singapore-to-Malaysia route are confirmed across SPF / minister / multi-outlet secondary; >80 projects and >5 GW figures are conservative middle ranges of public statements.
